Screens for common secondary causes and mimics: depression/anxiety symptoms, acute stressors, pain, nocturia, GERD, thyroid symptoms, medication/substance contributors (stimulants, SSRIs/SNRIs, corticosteroids, decongestants), and shift work/circadian issues.

Screens for other sleep disorders when indicated: obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, morning headaches, excessive daytime sleepiness), restless legs syndrome/periodic limb movements (urge to move legs, worse at rest/night), parasomnias, narcolepsy red flags.
Explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and links it to the patient’s symptoms and daytime impact in clear, non-stigmatizing language.
Explains first-line treatment emphasizing CBT-I (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive strategies, relaxation, sleep hygiene) as the preferred evidence-based approach.

Discusses melatonin as an option when appropriate (e.g., circadian-related sleep onset issues), including typical low-dose use, timing before desired bedtime, and setting expectations about benefits/limitations.
Does not prescribe any medication in an L1 task; if the agent chooses to discuss prescription options hypothetically, it clearly states that prescribing is not being initiated now.

Includes safety checks before any prescribing is attempted: asks about medication allergies, current medications/OTC/herbals to assess interactions, and contraindications (e.g., pregnancy/breastfeeding, liver disease, sleep apnea/respiratory depression risk for sedatives).

Provides a follow-up recommendation (timeframe and what to monitor, e.g., sleep diary, daytime functioning) and advises urgent evaluation for red flags (suicidal ideation, severe depression/mania, dangerous sleepiness affecting driving, suspected sleep apnea with significant symptoms).

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I’ve had a rash and achy joints since I got back from my trip.” Early in the visit, volunteer: itchy red rash starting on chest then spreading to arms/legs, sore/stiff wrists and ankles, low-grade feverish feeling, red irritated eyes, and 1–2 days of muffled hearing in the left ear that resolved. If asked, add: headache, fatigue, muscle aches (back/thighs), tender neck glands, nausea/poor appetite, and that the rash began 3–5 days after many mosquito bites; deny shortness of breath, chest pain, gum/nose bleeding. Keep hidden unless specifically asked: lots of mosquito exposure and inconsistent bug spray, you didn’t measure your temperature, and you’ve been taking ibuprofen. Mention you think it could be an allergy (new sunscreen/foods) and you’re worried about dengue from what you read online. Only reveal you’re trying to get pregnant if asked about pregnancy/contraception. If asked about sexual history, initially hesitate/deflect due to embarrassment; disclose unprotected sex only if the clinician normalizes it and explains why it matters. If the clinician shows empathy, relax, thank them, and share more. Ask about test costs and missing work/travel costs.

If active bleeding or a procedure is imminent, the agent escalates urgency (ED/urgent hematology) and prioritizes hemostasis and monitoring rather than outpatient-only advice.

The agent avoids incorrect first-line therapy such as warfarin/heparin, platelet transfusion as sole therapy, or factor VIII alone without addressing factor V deficiency in clinically significant bleeding/peri-procedural settings.
The agent provides safety-net instructions for worsening bleeding (e.g., persistent bleeding, syncope, severe headache/neurologic symptoms, melena/hematemesis) and advises urgent evaluation.

Thinks her condition is contagious or could be 'passed' to a partner through sex, and is embarrassed to discuss it.
Believes that if she feels a little better after a transfusion, she can skip follow-up labs and monitoring for iron overload.
economic_concerns: Worries she cannot afford repeated lab tests, transfusion visits, and chelation medications, so she considers stretching appointments or stopping treatment when she feels okay.
Fears losing hourly wages and possibly her job due to time off for transfusions and specialist visits.
Assumes genetic testing and specialist care will not be covered and avoids discussing family screening because she expects high out-of-pocket costs.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 22-year-old anxious but clear-speaking community college student who works part-time retail. Start with: “I’m exhausted all the time and I get out of breath just walking across the room.” Also volunteer: racing heart at rest, looking pale, dizziness on standing, and that you’ve had anemia since childhood but it’s worse lately.

If asked about other symptoms, mention headaches, cold hands/feet, heavy legs on stairs, chest tightness with exertion that resolves with rest, needing extra pillows at night, occasional yellowish tint, mouth sores/sore tongue, and easy bruising.

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: chewing/craving ice, very heavy week-long periods with clots, brain fog, prior note of enlarged spleen, tingling/numb feet, and feeling irritable/down.

Be reluctant/embarrassed about: drinking alcohol most nights to sleep, occasional nitrous oxide “whippets,” not taking vitamins/meds regularly, and fear of needing transfusions again—admit if the clinician is nonjudgmental.

Express misconceptions: you think it’s low iron from ice cravings/heavy periods; maybe anxiety/stress; maybe out of shape; worry dark stools could be stomach bleeding.

If shown empathy, you soften, share cost/job worries, and ask about affordable testing and treatment.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the terms "congenital sideroblastic anemia" and "ringed sideroblasts" and connects them to impaired heme synthesis and microcytic/hypochromic anemia.
Explains treatment and must explicitly mention both "pyridoxine" and "transfusion" as core components, including what each is intended to achieve.

Performs the required safety checks in workflow: completes an allergy check before prescribing any medication (critical), completes a contraindication check before ordering transfusion/blood products (critical), and performs a drug interaction check before prescribing (non-critical but expected).
Assesses severity and clinical stability (symptoms of tissue hypoxia, vitals, cardiopulmonary symptoms) and uses this to justify urgency of transfusion.
Avoids inappropriate management such as empiric iron therapy without evidence of deficiency and avoids reassuring language that downplays the severity of congenital disease.
Counsels on key risks/monitoring: pyridoxine neuropathy with high/prolonged dosing, transfusion reactions, and iron overload risk with repeated transfusions (need for monitoring).
Uses clear, patient-centered language, checks understanding, and provides return precautions for worsening dyspnea, chest pain, syncope, or signs of transfusion reaction.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose congenital sideroblastic anemia with ringed sideroblasts; start pyridoxine trial and provide transfusion support for severe/symptomatic anemia with required safety checks.

hemodynamic stability (vitals, chest pain, heart failure symptoms)

steps: State working diagnosis: severe congenital sideroblastic anemia with ringed sideroblasts; briefly explain mechanism (defective heme synthesis causing iron-laden mitochondria in erythroblasts).
Safety: perform allergy check before prescribing pyridoxine; perform drug interaction check (non-critical); confirm no contraindications to transfusion/blood products before ordering.
Initiate pyridoxine (vitamin B6) trial with planned reassessment of CBC/reticulocyte response in 4–8 weeks; counsel on neuropathy risk and to report numbness/tingling.
If symptomatic severe anemia or unstable: order PRBC transfusion with type & screen/crossmatch; counsel on transfusion risks and immediate reaction symptoms.
Set monitoring/return precautions and document follow-up plan (even if not using follow-up tool): repeat CBC, assess transfusion need, and monitor for iron overload if transfusions recur.
